text: I used to work on electrification projects.
I am used to managing suppliers in three time zones.
It took time, but I got used to reading CENELEC standards in English.
I would rather give you one real result than a list of skills.
Thank you for making the time, João. Let's cut to the chase, in about ninety seconds, tell me about yourself.

I'm a signaling engineer at Motiva. I used to work on electrification, and over three years I got used to owning the interface between design, installation and commissioning. Now I'm used to running that in English with German, French and Chinese suppliers.
Good, that is an arc, not a list. Now a behavioral question: tell me about a time you managed a serious conflict with an international supplier.

In 2024, six of forty units failed an EMC test, and the German supplier called it an installation problem. My task was to keep the design question open without stopping the line. I accepted the six in writing and put the shared grounding design on record, and as a result, they re-verified the configuration on all forty at their cost.
That is a clean STAR answer, and it is real. You have never worked on our platform, though. Read between the lines of the posting for me, why did we still invite you?

Because you hire for range, not for one platform, you value transferable skills and culture fit. Honestly, my one weakness is that I used to sell myself short in English. I'm getting used to describing my work as it really was, so I'll say it plainly: I owned that interface.
I appreciate that. The role is in Zurich, with relocation support, and we would rather hear a concern now than at handover. So, relocation, and your notice period?

I'm open to relocation, I got used to being deployed to site in 2023, and I'd rather build the next stage of my career where the projects are. My notice period is thirty days, so I could start early next quarter. And if I may say so, my selling point is exactly the interface you are hiring for.
I used to work on electrification, and I got used to owning the international interface.
I'm used to running technical calls in English, under real schedule pressure.
Let me give you a concrete example, using the STAR method.
That is a good question, let me think for a second.

Hello João, this is Claire Mercier from Rail Systems International. Thank you for your application for the Senior Signaling Engineer role. I have read your profile, and I would like to invite you to the next stage, so let me tell you what to expect. It will be a panel interview, three of us, about forty-five minutes, and it is competency-based. That means we will not ask you to list what you know. We will ask you to tell us about times when you actually did something: a supplier who let you down, a test that failed, a decision you had to make with incomplete information. The clearest way to answer those is the STAR method, and I would recommend you prepare two or three real examples before we speak. One more thing, and please read between the lines here. We are less interested in whether you have worked on our exact product, and more interested in whether your skills transfer and whether you are the right culture fit for the team. So do not sell yourself short because your background is electrification rather than our signaling platform. Finally, two practical points I need to raise early: the role is based in Zurich with relocation support, and I will need to understand your notice period. We can cover both at the end. I look forward to it. Speak soon.

Right, before your panel on Thursday, three things, because good engineers interview badly more often than anybody else, and it is nearly always the same three mistakes. First, you sell yourself short. You say I only did this, or I just helped with that, and the word only is doing terrible work in that sentence. You led the interface between three suppliers on a live commissioning. Say that. Nobody in the room will respect you more for shrinking it. Second, you answer the question you wish they had asked, not the one they asked. When they say tell me about a time you managed a conflict, they do not want your philosophy of conflict. They want one situation, one task, one action, one result. Cut to the chase, name the story in the first sentence, and let the STAR structure carry the rest. Third, and this is the one that separates a good candidate from the one who gets the offer: think on your feet, but do not rush. When they ask something you did not prepare, it is fine to say, that is a good question, let me think for a second. That pause makes you come across as calm and senior, not slow. Silence is not your enemy in an interview. Panic is. You have the track record. On Thursday, all you have to do is describe it like it belongs to you.
I used to work on electrification before I specialized in signaling.
I'm used to managing suppliers across three time zones.
It took time, but I got used to reading CENELEC standards in English.
I would rather give you one real result than a list of skills.
Let me give you a concrete example, using the STAR method.
A calm pause makes you come across as senior, not slow.
I used to work on electrification, and I got used to owning the international interface.
I'm used to managing suppliers across three time zones on a live commissioning.
I'm open to relocation, and my notice period is thirty days.
I'm used to running technical calls in English, under real schedule pressure.
That is a good question -- let me think for a second.
